text: The surgeon begins creating the initial peritoneal flap, carefully incising the peritoneum to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon observes the thin peritoneum on the lateral aspect of the patient's abdominal cavity, noting its delicate appearance.
The surgeon navigates within the pre-transversalis plane, revealing the anatomical layers and structures in this area.
The surgeon begins the dissection, developing a peritoneal flap and progressing medially towards the pubis.
The video shows a medial recurrence after an open repair, with visible anatomical structures indicating the location of the recurrence.
The surgical field shows a recurrence that appears to be in a direct space, but is actually located more medially than the typical direct space.
The surgeon focuses on the hernia sac, preparing to manipulate or reduce it as part of the procedure.
The surgeon encounters adhesions within the hernia sac, indicating areas where the tissue is abnormally attached.
The surgeon incises the transversalis fascia, which is adhered to the incarcerated fat, and begins the reduction of the herniated fat.
The surgeon is working on reducing a hernia, with approximately baseball-sized fat visible within the hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies the areolar fibers of the adhesions and carefully dissects them to reduce the herniated fat.
The surgeon has completed circumferential dissection and is now focusing on medial dissection, manipulating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs medial dissection around Cooper's ligament and the pubis, carefully separating tissue in this anatomical region.
The surgeon ensures thorough dissection two centimeters posterior to the previously identified landmark, maintaining clear separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon develops a space between the pubis and the bladder, carefully dissecting the tissue to create a clear separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon indicates the midline, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection, having crossed the midline, and proceeds with a more aggressive approach due to a very medial recurrence.
The surgeon performs contralateral dissection, focusing on the area opposite to the previously dissected medial region near Cooper's ligament and the pubis.
The surgeon develops the lateral flap, creating a visible separation between the tissue layers adjacent to the previously developed space between the pubis and the bladder.
The surgeon develops the lateral flap, focusing on the area lateral to the midline.

The surgeon adjusts the surgical field to accommodate the eventual placement of mesh. The prominent vas deferens and the distal aspect of the medial umbilical ligament are visible as the surgeon pulls medially to parietalize the cord elements.
The surgeon pulls on the tissue to achieve good retraction, providing clear exposure of the vas deferens. Electrocautery is used on surrounding inconsequential tissues.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord elements, manipulating the prominent vas deferens and surrounding structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the imaginary line between Cooper's ligament and the area of lateral dissection, highlighting the spatial orientation of these anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon continues the process of parietalization, manipulating tissue to achieve better exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes a prominent vas deferens, clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon pushes the tissue until it is lateralized, aligning it with the imaginary line between Cooper's ligament and the lateral dissection area.

The surgeon identifies other cord elements and notices a small hole in the thin peritoneum laterally. They grasp the tissue to develop a better lateral flap. A tubular structure is visible, identified as a medial ligament.
The surgeon begins dissection of Cooper's ligament, focusing on separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects towards the contralateral Cooper's ligament, ensuring the tissue is carefully separated and exposed.
The surgeon dissects in the direct space, focusing on separating tissue layers in this area.
The surgeon identifies the direct space, which is located more medially than the hernia in this patient.
The surgeon examines the hernia, which appears to be a combination of a former direct hernia, assessing its characteristics and location.
The surgeon dissects between the bladder and the pubis, creating a space approximately two centimeters wide to allow the mesh to lay flat medially and remain stable during bladder distension.
The surgeon begins dissection in the femoral space, carefully separating tissue layers to expose the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon inspects the femoral space, ensuring there are no femoral hernias present that could lead to future recurrence.
The surgeon parietalizes the spermatic vessels and vas deferens, ensuring they are appropriately separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon prepares to lay the mesh flat over the surgical area, ensuring that the anatomical structures are properly oriented with the mesh.
The surgeon examines the area lateral to the spermatic vessels to identify the iliopubic tract, confirming the absence of a cord lipoma.
The surgeon visualizes an imaginary line extending from the posterior aspect of Cooper's ligament beyond the lateral flap, aligning the surgical field for further steps.
The surgeon ensures that the peritoneal flap remains below the imaginary line extending from the posterior aspect of Cooper's ligament, maintaining proper orientation with the mesh.
The surgeon demonstrates fixation of the Cooper's ligament on the ipsilateral side, and also on the contralateral side for a medial recurrence.
The surgeon secures the mid-weight mesh to the Cooper's ligament, ensuring proper fixation for the surgical repair.
The surgeon secures the mesh twice directly underneath the hernia defect to ensure stability and prevent movement.
The surgeon secures the mesh twice underneath the hernia defect to ensure stability.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the contralateral Cooper's ligament due to the presence of a medial defect, ensuring stability in cases of direct or non-anatomic recurrence.
The surgeon performs a standard peritoneal flap closure at a high position, ensuring the flap is securely closed.
The surgeon proceeds with closing the defect, ensuring the mesh remains stable and properly positioned.
The surgeon brings the edges of the defect together, achieving a smooth closure of the tissue without visible tension or bleeding.
The surgeon placates the reduced hernia contents, which consist of somewhat heavy preperitoneal fat, ensuring the contents are securely positioned.
The surgeon brings the tissue edges together easily, indicating minimal tension in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a few sutures to placate the herniated contents, reducing the potential for these contents to exert pressure on the mesh.
